Plan the change
Carry out a small scale study
Review and analyze the results, identify what you have learned
Take action. Incorporate the change into the configuration
Technical PDCA cycle

ACM
Plan
Act
The purpose of this exercise is to help the ACM consultant understand what s/he must do when
Assess Sponsorship in a structured interview:
What does success look like to them personally?
What are they willing to do visibly?
What resources will they defend when competing priorities emerge?
How have they handled change resistance before?
It's possible to use a sponsor readiness matrix (Prosci's CLARC model, which tracks devekopment of sponsorship over time)

Stakeholder Identification and Influence Mapping:
Structured Interviews with Senior leaders (and their direct reports). Influence can be identified by asking the question: When something needs to get done here that isn't straight-forward, who do people actually go to? Document this as a stakeholder map with axes of influence and disposition (supportive, neutral, resistant)
Impact analysis at the role level: This is where we need to understand how specific roles will change. Workflows will disappear, which gain new steps which require fundamentally different skills. The tool here is a structured impact analysis, typically a matrix. Mapping the gap between the current workflow and the future state workflow per role is the raw material for everything that's designed later.
Assess current state capability baseline: What can people actually do right now with their existing tools? What are their current abilities and frustrations? Surveys can work here.
Organisational Change History and Scar Tissue: Understand the narrative of previous changes, especially the ones that went badly. Interview long-tenured staff and middle managers, asking specifically what changed in the last two to three years, what went well, and what was painful. What was promised and what was not delivered.
Assess middle management readiness and willingness: This determines whether the change actually propagates or dies. Possible to go into small group conversations.
Get technical deployment timeline and dependencies. Map every milestone that has a downstream ACM implication. Will the pilot environments be available? The tenant configuration is finalised. The rollback plan if Go Live fails Build the plan against the technical timeline and make sure to be apprised of changes.
Create Resistance Risk Profile. For each affected population you should be able to articulate what they stand to lose in terms of:
- status
- competence
- autonomy
- workload
- predictability
and what they might gain.What does their prior change experience predispose them toward? Which structural factors, like manager support, sponsor visibility, communication trust, either amplify or dampen their resistance? This profile should drive the intervention design.
Establishing a diagnostic baseline (2-4) weeks
Do
Create a change strategy document. Put the findings into a coherent argument: Here's the organisation we're changing. Here's what makes this change specifically difficult for them. Here's what we'll do about it and here's how we'll know how it's working. It needs to contain named populations with specific intervention plans.
Design a stakeholder engagement plan. Different from the communication plan and operates at a different altitude. Build a coalition before the change becomes visible to the broader population.  Your one-on-one conversations with key influencers. Supportive leaders with talking points and early access Engage resistance stakeholders early enough. Their concerns can shape the approach rather than ambush it. Stakeholder engagement plans should have a cadence:
- Who to meet with
- When
- What you need from them at each stage
- What they need from you
Build communication with message architecture:
Messages need to be sequenced in a way that matches the audience's psychological journey not the project's technical milestones.
First communication: answer why? Why this change? Why now? Why us?
Second communication: What will change specifically for me?
Third communication: We address the how to use the new thing.
Managers should hear about things before the teams do.
Design the training architecture. (not the training content yet.)
Which modality is for which populations? What points in the deployment timeline with what prerequisites measured by outcome. This is obvious. A finance team needs deep training in Co-Pilot in Excel, while communications need Word and Outlook.
Establish the measurement framework. Focus on adoption metrics as the first level; that is, utilisation telemetry. Second-level proficiency metrics assessment scores, task completion, quality, error rates. Third level: Business impact metrics. Output quality indicators. You need Partnerships with business stakeholders here.
Put these measurements into your change strategy document.
Develop resistance management approach. In the diagnostic was a resistance risk profile. This needs to be operationalized. For each resistance cluster identified, design a specific intervention and be precise about the mechanism.
Example: If resistance is rooted in competence, anxiety (people fear looking incompetent withg the new tool) Intervention is safe practise environments with psychological safety. Not more communications about how great the feature is.
Example 2: If resistance is structural, for example the change is generally making someone's job harder without a compensating benefit, the intervention might be escalating that finding to governance as a design problem rather than an adoption problem. The resistance management approach should:
1. Name the pattern
2. Attribute the cause
3. Specify the intervention
4. Assign ownership
Set up the change network. Identify and recruit change agents like champions. It should be people who carry peer credibility and can serve as local translators for the change.
The set-up work involves:
- formal recruitment
- asking them
- explaining the role
- securing their manager's support for the time commitment
Equipping: give them early access, give them deeper context, and a direct line back to you for intelligence.
Establish a rhythm for check-in. These should be regular check-ins where they report ground-level sentiment and you provide them with updated talking points and troubleshooting guidance. This change network becomes the distributing sensing apparatus during deployment.
Negotiate governance integration. Make sure that ACM has a formal seat in the programme's governance structure. Which decisions can an ACM lead make autonomously? What constitutes an ACM risk that triggers escalation.
Designing the route | activate Champions
Deploy communication + training
Activate the communication sequence. Communicate at least 48 to 72 hours before anything goes live. It's far enough ahead to allow questions to surface through the management layer.
Also, what are the champions getting back? Identify message failures that need immediate correction.
Execute training as per the architecture. Awareness first, then skill building, then applied practise.
Champion network Shift from preparation mode to active field support. They provide peer-level help that carries less stigma than asking IT support or attending other trainings. They collect ground-level intelligence about what's working and what's confusing. They model productive use visibly within their teams.
Your job is to maintain the cadence of Champion Check-ins, daily or every other day during the first two weeks of deployment.
Only intelligence they provide within hours not days.
Run the support infrastructure. Whatever model has been designed:
- a dedicated help desk queue
- drop-in clinics or floor walking support
- Teams channel for questions
It needs to be visibly operational from the moment access goes live.
People who hit a problem and find nothing will form a narrative of abandonment that persists long after support improves. Over-resource the first week deliberately then scale back on demand data.
Monitor adoption telemetry against the baseline.
Conduct active Resistance Management. Pre-identified resistance clusters will now manifest as behaviour.
Distinguish between productive resistance (People identifying genuine problems that should be escalated to the technical team as design feedback) And defensive (resistance, people protecting established habits or status.)

Prerequisite items:
(aske their internal project lead for:
List of polulations
List of ppl involved, their function, department and email
Set up dedicated Teams channels:
-Sponsors
- Pilot participants
- Champions
- Managers
Identify the one (plus backup) person that will keep on engaging champions beyond consulting horizon and will check up on sustaining that change. Who "carries the banner?"
Manage the sponsor visibly. The sponsor needs to be active and visible at least first two weeks during deployment.
Planning phase1
Planning phase 2
Run rapid Check Act loops
A few days during deployment synthesise what you're seeing across all sensing channels. By two or three most significant gaps between expected and actual adoption Diagnose probable causes, design adjustments, and implement them within the same week. Document what you changed and why in a log
Check
Analyse telemetry data
Do deployment interviews. What changed? It's harder. What did you stop doing that you used to do? What do you wish you could do that you can't figure out?
Evaluate training against against actual performance
Assess Champion network level health. Conduct structured review: who is still active? And how is their own adoption level?
Run programme level check. A strategic review that synthesises everything:
- telemetry
- interview findings
- training effectiveness
- analysis
- champion reports
- support ticket trends
- resistance pattern evolution
- sponsor engagement quality
into a coherent assessment of where adoption stands against the success criteria that were defined in the plan phase. This final assessment should go to the steering committee as a formal deliverable not as a verbal update because it is the evidence base for continued resourcing.
The reinforcement plan. Suggest advanced training for cohorts ready to deepen. Remedial support for cohorts still struggling with the basics.
Systemic barriers that training cannot fix. Diagnostic and deployment experience will have surface problems that are not adoption problems at all. They are design, policy, or infrastructure problems wearing the clothing of adoption. The security configuration is so restrictive that Co-Pilot can't access the data people actually need, making it functionally useless for certain workflows. Perhaps a business process was never designed/redesigned to accommodate the new capability so people are using an AI tool to automate a process that actually shouldn't exist.
These systemic barriers Need to be escalated to their respective owners with specific evidence and proposed resolutions.
Begin knowledghe transfer. Identify internal ownership for ongoing adoption management. Typically a combination of:
- IT for telemetry and technical support
- learning and development for continued training
- a business side adoption owner for strategic direction
Start transferring your methods, measurement framework, champion network management approach, and your stakeholder relationships to those internal owners.
State resistance profile and adjust. Resistance may have resolved or transformed into something more specific. New resistance patterns may have emerged as people discover the tool's actual limitations versus its marketed capabilities. Appointment-driven resistance is qualitatively different from fear-driven resistance and requires a different response. Resistance will have proven valid. Two genuine problems that the organisation should address; see also the part about systemic barriers.
Codify what worked into the organisational infrastructure. That is, where adoption succeeded specific cohorts, specific workflows, specific use patterns that demonstrably produce value. The job here is to make that success structurally permanent rather than dependent on continued project energy. - Embedding the new ways of working into formal processes
- Updated standard operating procedures
- Revised job descriptions that could include the new competencies
- Onboarding programmes for new hires that assume the tool as baseline rather than treating it as an additionIt means ensuring the training materials that were developed transition from project artefacts into the organisation's learning management system with an assigned owner and refresh cycle. The test here is simple: If every person associated with the change project left tomorrow, would the successful adoption patterns persist? If the answer is no, you haven't finished this phase; you've merely sustained the previous phase longer.
Create an ACM programme retrospective. Were the interventions correct? What went well? What went not so well? Make a list of what you take to the next programme. Identify the time that each of these parts took in order to better understand the scope of the next project.
